description: Networking and inter-application communication in TouchDesigner including OSC server/client, WebSocket (client via WebSocket DAT, server via Web Server DAT), NDI video streaming, Ableton Live integration (TDAbleton / AbletonOSC), and direct TD-to-TD links via Touch In/Out.

techniques.name: OSC Server and Client
techniques.description: Open Sound Control (OSC) communication in TouchDesigner for sending and receiving parameter data, triggers, and messages between applications, instruments, and machines.
techniques.notes: OSC In CHOP receives continuous float values as CHOP channels. OSC In DAT receives any OSC message as table rows. OSC Out CHOP/DAT sends data. Common ports: 7000 (receive), 7001 (send) — these are conventions, not fixed defaults. Firewall must allow UDP on the chosen ports. On the OSC Out DAT the destination is the 'address' parameter (par.address); the OSC In DAT/CHOP listen on 'port' and bind to an interface via 'localaddress'.

code.snippet:
# OSC Setup and Handling in TouchDesigner

# --- OSC Receiver Setup ---
def setup_osc_receiver(oscInDat, port=7000, local_address='0.0.0.0'):
    """
    Configure OSC In DAT to receive on specified port.
    Messages arrive as table rows; the onReceiveOSC callback also fires per message.
    """
    oscInDat.par.port = port
    oscInDat.par.localaddress = local_address  # interface to bind; '0.0.0.0' = all
    oscInDat.par.active = True
    print(f'OSC receiving on port {port}')

# --- OSC Sender Setup ---
def setup_osc_sender(oscOutDat, host='127.0.0.1', port=7001):
    """
    Configure OSC Out DAT to send to specified host:port.
    NOTE: the destination host parameter is 'address' (NOT 'networkaddress').
    """
    oscOutDat.par.address = host
    oscOutDat.par.port = port
    print(f'OSC sending to {host}:{port}')

# --- Sending OSC Messages ---
def send_osc(oscOutDat, address, *values):
    """
    Send an OSC message with address and values.
    Values can be float, int, or string.
    oscoutDAT.sendOSC() signature: sendOSC(address, *values, asBundle=False)
    Values are passed as separate positional args (unpacked), NOT as a list.
    """
    oscOutDat.sendOSC(address, *values)

# Example sends
# send_osc(op('osc_out1'), '/td/position', 0.5, 0.3, 1.0)
# send_osc(op('osc_out1'), '/td/trigger', 1)
# send_osc(op('osc_out1'), '/td/label', 'hello')
# Direct call (one message): op('osc_out1').sendOSC('/td/position', 0.5, 0.3, 1.0)

# --- Receiving OSC Messages (onReceiveOSC callback) ---
# Place in the Callbacks DAT referenced by the OSC In DAT's 'callbacks' parameter.
# Verified signature (docs.derivative.ca/OscinDAT_Class):
def onReceiveOSC(dat, rowIndex, message, bytes, timeStamp, address, args, peer):
    """
    Called for each incoming OSC message.
    rowIndex: row the message was placed at in the DAT table
    message:  ASCII representation of the message
    bytes:    raw byte array of the message
    timeStamp: OSC time tag component
    address:  OSC address string, e.g. '/td/control'
    args:     list of argument values
    peer:     Peer object (peer.address, peer.port, peer.owner)
    """
    # Route messages to different handlers
    if address.startswith('/td/position'):
        if len(args) >= 3:
            op('pos_control').par.value0 = float(args[0])
            op('pos_control').par.value1 = float(args[1])
            op('pos_control').par.value2 = float(args[2])
    
    elif address.startswith('/td/color'):
        if len(args) >= 3:
            op('bg_color').par.colorr = float(args[0])
            op('bg_color').par.colorg = float(args[1])
            op('bg_color').par.colorb = float(args[2])
    
    elif address.startswith('/td/trigger'):
        op('trigger_node').par.value0.pulse()
    
    # Log all messages for debugging
    print(f'[OSC] {address}: {args} from {peer.address}:{peer.port}')

# --- Bidirectional OSC with TouchOSC or similar ---
def setup_touchosc_bridge(oscInDat, oscOutDat, tablet_ip, receive_port=8000, send_port=9000):
    """
    Configure for TouchOSC app communication.
    """
    setup_osc_receiver(oscInDat, receive_port)
    setup_osc_sender(oscOutDat, tablet_ip, send_port)
    print(f'TouchOSC bridge: receive={receive_port}, send to {tablet_ip}:{send_port}')

techniques.name: WebSocket: Client (WebSocket DAT) and Server (Web Server DAT)
techniques.description: Real-time bidirectional communication between TouchDesigner and web browsers / Node.js servers. The WebSocket DAT is a CLIENT (it connects out to a WebSocket server). To act as a SERVER that browsers connect to, use the Web Server DAT, which serves HTTP and accepts WebSocket connections.
techniques.notes: Important: the WebSocket DAT only operates as a CLIENT — it connects to an external server at its 'netaddress'/'port' parameters. There is no server mode on the WebSocket DAT. For TD to be the server that browsers connect to (ws://this-machine:PORT), use the Web Server DAT and its WebSocket callbacks (onWebSocketOpen/Close/ReceiveText/ReceiveBinary). The Web Server DAT targets individual clients with webSocketSendText(client, data); the WebSocket DAT (client) sends with sendText(message).

code.snippet:
# WebSocket SERVER in TouchDesigner -> use the Web Server DAT.
# Browsers connect to ws://<this-machine-ip>:<port>
# (The WebSocket DAT is a CLIENT only and cannot accept incoming connections.)

# Web Server DAT parameters:
#   Active: On
#   Port: 9090
# Set the 'callbacks' parameter to a Text/Callbacks DAT containing the functions below.

# --- Callbacks DAT for the Web Server DAT ---
# Verified signatures (docs.derivative.ca/WebserverDAT_Class):

def onWebSocketOpen(webServerDAT, client, uri):
    """
    Called when a client opens a WebSocket connection.
    client: opaque client identifier (string) used to target sends
    uri:    the requested URI
    """
    print(f'[WS] Client connected: {client} ({uri})')
    # Send a welcome message to just this client
    webServerDAT.webSocketSendText(client,
        '{"type":"welcome","message":"Connected to TouchDesigner"}')

def onWebSocketClose(webServerDAT, client):
    print(f'[WS] Client disconnected: {client}')

def onWebSocketReceiveText(webServerDAT, client, data):
    """
    Called when text is received from a client.
    data: the text payload (str)
    """
    import json
    try:
        msg = json.loads(data)
        msg_type = msg.get('type', '')
        
        if msg_type == 'control':
            # Map incoming data to TD parameters
            if 'x' in msg: op('control').par.value0 = float(msg['x'])
            if 'y' in msg: op('control').par.value1 = float(msg['y'])
            if 'color' in msg:
                rgb = msg['color']
                op('bg_color').par.colorr = rgb[0] / 255.0
                op('bg_color').par.colorg = rgb[1] / 255.0
                op('bg_color').par.colorb = rgb[2] / 255.0
        
        elif msg_type == 'trigger':
            op('trigger_event').par.value0.pulse()
        
        # Echo back acknowledgment to the sending client
        webServerDAT.webSocketSendText(client,
            json.dumps({'type': 'ack', 'received': msg_type}))
    
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        print(f'[WS] Invalid JSON: {data}')

def onWebSocketReceiveBinary(webServerDAT, client, data):
    print(f'[WS] Binary from {client}: {len(data)} bytes')

# Optional: serve a plain HTTP page from the same Web Server DAT.
def onHTTPRequest(webServerDAT, request, response):
    response['statusCode'] = 200
    response['statusReason'] = 'OK'
    response['data'] = '<html><body>TouchDesigner Web Server</body></html>'
    return response

# --- WebSocket CLIENT (WebSocket DAT) ---
# Use this when TD must connect OUT to an existing WebSocket server.
def setup_websocket_client(wsDat, host='echo.websocket.events', port=80):
    """
    Configure the WebSocket DAT (client) to connect to a remote server.
    The connection address parameter is 'netaddress' (NOT 'networkaddress').
    """
    wsDat.par.netaddress = host
    wsDat.par.port = port
    wsDat.par.active = True

# WebSocket DAT (client) callbacks live in its Callbacks DAT.
# Verified signatures (docs.derivative.ca/WebsocketDAT_Class):
#   def onConnect(dat): ...
#   def onDisconnect(dat): ...
#   def onReceiveText(dat, rowIndex, message): ...
#   def onReceiveBinary(dat, contents): ...
# Send from the client with: wsDat.sendText('hello')

# --- JavaScript client example (browser connecting to the Web Server DAT) ---
# const ws = new WebSocket('ws://localhost:9090');
# ws.onopen = () => ws.send(JSON.stringify({type:'control', x:0.5, y:0.3}));
# ws.onmessage = (e) => console.log('TD:', JSON.parse(e.data));

techniques.name: NDI Video Streaming
techniques.description: Network Device Interface (NDI) for low-latency video over LAN. Send and receive full-quality video streams between TouchDesigner instances, video mixers, and other NDI-compatible software.
techniques.notes: NDI is created by NewTek (now part of Vizrt). NDI typically requires devices on the same network segment; cross-subnet discovery needs Extra Search IPs (the 'extraips' parameter) or NDI discovery/bridge tooling. The NDI DAT lists/monitors available sources on the network. On the NDI Out TOP the broadcast name is the 'name' parameter and groups are configured via the 'grouptable' parameter (a DAT reference), not a plain string. On the NDI In TOP the source is selected via the 'name' parameter. NDI is near-zero latency on a GigE LAN.

code.snippet:
# NDI Streaming Setup and Management

def list_ndi_sources(ndiDat):
    """
    NDI DAT lists available NDI sources found on the network.
    The table has a header row; column layout depends on the build, so we
    read by header name when possible and fall back to the first column.
    Returns a list of source-name strings.
    """
    sources = []
    name_col = ndiDat.col('name')  # None if no such header
    if name_col is not None:
        # cells in the header column, skipping the header row itself
        for cell in name_col[1:]:
            sources.append(str(cell.val))
    else:
        for row in range(1, ndiDat.numRows):  # skip header row
            sources.append(str(ndiDat[row, 0].val))
    for s in sources:
        print(f'[NDI] Found source: {s}')
    return sources

def setup_ndi_receiver(ndiInTop, source_name):
    """
    Configure NDI In TOP to receive from a named source.
    The source parameter is 'name' (e.g. 'WORKSTATION (Program)').
    """
    ndiInTop.par.name = source_name
    ndiInTop.par.active = True
    print(f'[NDI] Receiving: {source_name}')

def setup_ndi_sender(ndiOutTop, stream_name='TouchDesigner Output'):
    """
    Configure NDI Out TOP to broadcast on the network.
    The broadcast name is the 'name' parameter.
    (Group membership, if needed, is set via the 'grouptable' parameter,
     which references a DAT listing group names.)
    """
    ndiOutTop.par.name = stream_name
    ndiOutTop.par.active = True
    print(f'[NDI] Broadcasting as: {stream_name}')

def auto_connect_ndi(ndiDat, ndiInTop, preferred_source_keyword='VMIX'):
    """
    Auto-connect to first available NDI source matching keyword.
    """
    sources = list_ndi_sources(ndiDat)
    for name in sources:
        if preferred_source_keyword.lower() in name.lower():
            setup_ndi_receiver(ndiInTop, name)
            return True
    if sources:
        setup_ndi_receiver(ndiInTop, sources[0])
        return True
    print('[NDI] No sources available')
    return False

# Multi-instance NDI routing (e.g., stage distribution)
def setup_stage_distribution(outputs):
    """
    Configure multiple NDI Out TOPs for stage distribution.
    outputs: list of (ndiOutTop, stream_name) tuples
    """
    for ndiOutTop, stream_name in outputs:
        setup_ndi_sender(ndiOutTop, stream_name)

techniques.name: TDAbleton Live Integration
techniques.description: Bidirectional communication between TouchDesigner and Ableton Live using TDAbleton, Derivative's official toolkit. TDAbleton talks to Live through Ableton's MIDI Remote Scripts (with Max for Live devices where needed) and exposes the data in TouchDesigner via dedicated components — you do NOT write raw OSC addresses by hand.
techniques.notes: TDAbleton ships with TouchDesigner (Palette > Tools > TDAbleton). It installs a Remote Script into Ableton Live's MIDI Remote Scripts and communicates with TD over OSC (UDP) under the hood, but you interact with it through components: the tdAbleton COMP holds the connection, and abletonSong / abletonTrack / abletonParameter / abletonLevel / abletonMIDI components surface tempo, transport, clip, device-parameter and level/spectrum data as CHOP/DAT channels you can reference directly. Do NOT hand-write '/live/...' OSC addresses against TDAbleton — those are the AbletonOSC convention (a separate, third-party remote script), not how TDAbleton works. For tempo sync WITHOUT TDAbleton, the Ableton Link CHOP speaks the Ableton Link protocol and auto-discovers Link peers on the LAN.

code.snippet:
# TDAbleton Integration (official Derivative toolkit)
#
# Setup (no hand-written OSC):
#   1. In Live: Preferences > Link/Tempo/MIDI, add the TDAbleton Remote Script
#      that the TDAbleton component installs (it copies a script into Live's
#      'MIDI Remote Scripts' folder and the matching Max for Live devices).
#   2. In TD: drag the TDAbleton component from the Palette
#      (Palette > Tools > TDAbleton). It creates a 'tdAbleton' COMP that
#      manages the connection.
#   3. Add the per-feature components you need (abletonSong, abletonTrack,
#      abletonParameter, abletonLevel, abletonMIDI ...). Each exposes Live
#      data as channels/rows you reference like any other operator.
#
# You read/write Live through these components, NOT by sending raw OSC.

class TDAbletonBridge:
    """
    Thin convenience wrapper around TDAbleton components.
    Pass in the relevant component operators created by the TDAbleton palette
    item, e.g. op('abletonSong1'), op('abletonTrack1').
    """
    def __init__(self, abletonSong, abletonTrack=None):
        self.song = abletonSong      # abletonSong COMP
        self.track = abletonTrack    # abletonTrack COMP (optional)

    # --- Reading Live state (data is published as CHOP channels) ---
    def get_tempo(self):
        """abletonSong exposes a 'tempo' channel on its output CHOP."""
        ch = self.song.op('out1')  # the component's data CHOP
        if ch is not None and 'tempo' in [c.name for c in ch.chans()]:
            return ch['tempo'].eval()
        return 120.0

    def is_playing(self):
        ch = self.song.op('out1')
        if ch is not None and 'is_playing' in [c.name for c in ch.chans()]:
            return bool(ch['is_playing'].eval())
        return False

    # --- Controlling Live ---
    # TDAbleton components expose parameters and pulse parameters that drive
    # Live. For example the abletonSong component has Start/Stop pulse pars:
    def play(self):
        # Pulse the component's transport-start parameter (name varies by
        # TD version; inspect the abletonSong component's parameters).
        if hasattr(self.song.par, 'Startplaying'):
            self.song.par.Startplaying.pulse()

    def stop(self):
        if hasattr(self.song.par, 'Stopplaying'):
            self.song.par.Stopplaying.pulse()

    def set_tempo(self, bpm):
        # abletonSong typically exposes a settable Tempo parameter.
        if hasattr(self.song.par, 'Tempo'):
            self.song.par.Tempo = float(bpm)

# NOTE: exact parameter / channel names on TDAbleton components can change
# between TD builds. Inspect the components in your installed version (right-
# click > Parameters, or view the output CHOP/DAT channels) rather than
# assuming fixed names. See https://docs.derivative.ca/TDAbleton

# --- Ableton Link CHOP — tempo sync without TDAbleton ---
# Just add an Ableton Link CHOP; it auto-discovers Link sessions on the LAN
# and outputs 'tempo' and 'beat' channels (among others: phase, bar, etc.).
def get_link_tempo(abletonLinkChop):
    """Read current Ableton Link tempo."""
    if 'tempo' in [c.name for c in abletonLinkChop.chans()]:
        return abletonLinkChop['tempo'][0]
    return 120.0

def get_link_beat(abletonLinkChop):
    """Read current beat position from Ableton Link."""
    if 'beat' in [c.name for c in abletonLinkChop.chans()]:
        return abletonLinkChop['beat'][0]
    return 0.0

techniques.name: AbletonOSC Integration (third-party OSC remote script)
techniques.description: Controlling Ableton Live from TouchDesigner using AbletonOSC, a separate open-source MIDI Remote Script that exposes Live via plain '/live/...' OSC addresses. This is NOT TDAbleton — it requires installing AbletonOSC into Ableton Live, then driving it with TD's standard OSC In/Out DAT/CHOP.
techniques.notes: AbletonOSC (github.com/ideoforms/AbletonOSC) is an open-source Remote Script you copy into Ableton Live's 'Remote Scripts' folder and enable as a Control Surface. By default it LISTENS on UDP 11000 and REPLIES on UDP 11001. Its address space is '/live/song/...', '/live/clip_slot/...', '/live/track/...', etc. Because it is just OSC, you talk to it with TouchDesigner's normal OSC In/Out operators. This is a different project from Derivative's TDAbleton — do not mix the two address conventions. Verify the exact addresses and ports against the AbletonOSC README for the version you install (they have changed across releases).

techniques.name: Touch In / Touch Out for TD-to-TD Networking
techniques.description: Using Touch In / Touch Out CHOP, DAT, and TOP operators for direct communication between TouchDesigner instances over a LAN, without OSC overhead.
techniques.notes: Touch In/Out uses streaming TCP/IP by default (the CHOP/DAT also offer UDP messaging protocols). The Touch Out operator is the SERVER (it transmits on a 'port'); the Touch In operator is the CLIENT and connects to the server's 'address'. The connect parameter is 'address' (NOT 'networkaddress'). Touch In/Out exists for CHOP, DAT and TOP data. The Touch Out TOP supports Hap Q / Hap Q Alpha / Uncompressed codecs. Ensure Windows Firewall is unblocked for TouchDesigner.

code.snippet:
# Touch In/Out Networking Setup
# Touch Out = server (listens on a port). Touch In = client (connects to address).

def setup_touch_out_server(touchOutOp, port=7500):
    """
    Configure a Touch Out CHOP/DAT/TOP as the server (transmitter).
    Remote machines' Touch In operators connect to THIS machine's IP + port.
    The server only needs a port; it does not 'connect' anywhere.
    """
    touchOutOp.par.port = port
    touchOutOp.par.active = True
    print(f'Touch Out serving on port {port}')

def setup_touch_in_client(touchInOp, server_ip, port=7500):
    """
    Configure a Touch In CHOP/DAT/TOP (client) to receive from a server.
    server_ip: IP/host of the master TouchDesigner machine.
    The connect parameter is 'address' (NOT 'networkaddress').
    """
    touchInOp.par.address = server_ip
    touchInOp.par.port = port
    touchInOp.par.active = True
    print(f'Touch In connecting to {server_ip}:{port}')

# Multi-machine show setup
def configure_show_network(machine_role, server_ip='192.168.1.100'):
    """
    Configure network based on machine role.
    'master': sends data (Touch Out servers).
    'client': receives data (Touch In clients).
    """
    if machine_role == 'master':
        setup_touch_out_server(op('touch_out_chop'))
        setup_touch_out_server(op('touch_out_top'), port=7501)
        print('Master: broadcasting CHOP and TOP data')
    else:
        setup_touch_in_client(op('touch_in_chop'), server_ip)
        setup_touch_in_client(op('touch_in_top'), server_ip, port=7501)
        print(f'Client: receiving from master at {server_ip}')
